collect.intro: Bring cards, mobile money, and wallets onto one payment rail, with business rules that match how you manage cash in.
collect.p1: The partner portal is the control plane: you define channels, currencies, and rules, then follow each inflow to settlement in one place, without a patchwork of reconciliation tools.
collect.p2: Dashboards and webhooks feed your own systems with amounts, fees, and statuses, so product and finance share the same facts.

bullets.b1: A single flow for all accepted methods
bullets.b2: Clear settlement rules tied to your plan
bullets.b3: Shared visibility for product and finance teams

collect.outro: Need to shape recurring, baskets, or refunds? We can help align flows with your volume and policy.

paymentLinks.intro: Send a pay link when a full checkout integration is not the priority: campaigns, quotes, follow-ups, or light-touch use cases.
paymentLinks.p1: You create a link, share it by email, SMS, or social; the customer pays with the same methods enabled for your account, and everything shows up in the same back office as your API traffic.
paymentLinks.p2: A practical bridge before you ship a custom front, or for occasional, easy-to-explain collection moments.

bullets.b1: Get started without a heavy front-end build
bullets.b2: Same rails and rules as a full integration
bullets.b3: Unified status history with the rest of your business

paymentLinks.outro: If you need branded URLs or payment schedules, we can review fit with your scenario and compliance.

massPayouts.intro: Run one-off or batch payouts to mobile money and wallets, with per-beneficiary traceability for operations and review.
massPayouts.p1: Upload, API, or port-based launches keep every line item identifiable: beneficiary, status, and reason, without losing context in a spreadsheet.
massPayouts.p2: Hooks into your own tooling help you retry, escalate, and alert without silent delays.

bullets.b1: Batches or single sends, as you need
bullets.b3: Aligned with the same partner account as your collections

massPayouts.outro: Payroll, partners, or suppliers: we work with you on the right KYC/AML scope for your programme.

schoolFees.intro: Make school, club, or institution collections predictable, with a clear pay journey and history for the organiser.
schoolFees.p1: Payers get a simple flow; you track campaigns, reminders, and balances without a parallel tangle of spreadsheets for received amounts.
schoolFees.p2: Start from the web portal, then add API when enrolment and reporting volume make it worth it.

bullets.b1: Guided pay journey and proof of payment for payers
bullets.b2: Tracking by cohort, class, or member
bullets.b3: Consistent with your other online collection channels

schoolFees.outro: Tell us about your collection calendar and we can shape reminder timing with you.

depositWithdraw.intro: Move working capital between the partner wallet, mobile money, and the bank, with less manual back-and-forth for reconciliation.
depositWithdraw.p1: Movements are logged in the same system as the rest of your payment activity, which helps daily balance checks and bank matching.
depositWithdraw.p2: You tune limits and allowed rails to match your governance, without re-architecting the entire stack.

bullets.b2: Fewer exports just to know where the money sits
bullets.b3: Reporting that lines up with your collection story

depositWithdraw.outro: Arrival times depend on rails and banks: we are transparent on operational cut-offs per method.

apiSuite.intro: Connect to Smallpay with documented HTTP endpoints, webhooks, and logs, across ERP, order services, and internal automations.
apiSuite.p1: Partners usually validate in a sandbox, then go live with the same event model (success, failure, chargebacks) for operations.
apiSuite.p2: OpenAPI, sample cURL, and the same vocabulary as the web portal, so you do not re-label statuses in every script.

bullets.b1: Shared semantics for portal and API
bullets.b2: Webhooks to drive your internal playbooks (alerts, inventory)
bullets.b3: Logs and exports your accounting team can work with

apiSuite.outro: Need a connector to SAP or NetSuite? We map events first, then the integration details.

f1.title: How to choose a payment aggregator
f1.teaser: From pricing to API support, from data residency to the breadth of payment methods—the criteria that separate a throwaway connection from a long-term payment partner.
f1.imageAlt: Abstract illustration: choosing an aggregator (network diagram)

f2.title: FinTech trends in Africa
f2.teaser: Open banking, mobile money, and market integrations: what’s accelerating, what inclusion and infrastructure need, and what to plan for in 2026.

f3.title: Securing online payments
f3.teaser: 3-D Secure, SCA, risk controls, monitoring, and practical patterns that reassure customers and partners without ruining the checkout experience.

p1.title: Partner API integration: auth, webhooks, and errors
p1.author: Nadia K.
p1.excerpt: Launching a payment API integration is not just about writing requests: you need environments, keys, fallbacks, and just enough observability from the first token. Start with an isolated sandbox, version your contracts, and lock in a set of test accounts that cover success, decline, timeout, and chargeback. Token issuance, refresh, idempotency headers, and correlation IDs all need clear failure policy: client-side retries with backoff, circuit breakers, and structured logs for support. Webhooks are not a side product: verify signatures, handle duplicates, acknowledge quickly, and do not make irreversible actions from headers alone. On sensitive paths, expect status races, partial replays, and out-of-order events. On the product side, expose full transaction state in the back office, not only at checkout. On operations, map merchant references to processor IDs, keep an audit of manual adjustments, and have a reversion runbook for hosting outages. Finally, key rotation, role separation, and debug data hygiene matter: a production-grade integration is one that another engineer can re-run, test, and debug under pressure with written steps.

p2.title: African FinTech: local rails, inclusion, 2026 outlook
p2.author: Boris M.
p2.excerpt: African fintech is built on heterogeneous rails: mobile money, cards, instant transfers, and increasingly account-to-account bridges. For an aggregator, the game is reliable routing, comparable pricing, and reporting that still respects different accounting realities—while network volatility, opportunistic fraud, and micro-merchant inclusion all pull in different directions. Convergence to interoperable standards and banking partnerships does not remove the need for orchestration: you still need something that hides heterogeneity, watches technical “branches,” and simplifies onboarding. Merchants are asking for finer subscription, discounting, and settlement engines, while regulators stress traceability, AML, and the careful hosting of sensitive data. 2026 innovation trends include multi-currency orchestration with FX control, smarter reconciliation, and onboarding flows aimed at teams with uneven IT skills. On impact, inclusion is not a postscript: it is an adoption driver, measurable in time-to-credit, per-transaction cost, and the perceived ease for the end beneficiary, from the local shop to the scale-up that exports across the region.

p3.title: Comparing aggregators: a field guide (without the noise)
p3.author: Hélène D.
p3.excerpt: Fee grids, hidden costs, implementation effort, and support quality: a simple framework to run an RFP, with a checklist for merchants and integrators.

quote2.text: We wanted finance-friendly reporting without building a data warehouse: status granularity was the dealbreaker.

httpApiDoc.introLead: The server URL below matches servers[0] in the contract (e.g. local gateway). Replace it with your deployment URL.
httpApiDoc.authIntro: The specification defines several authentication modes:
httpApiDoc.authJwtNote: After POST /auth or POST /auth/token, send the JWT on protected routes (security: bearerAuth).
httpApiDoc.authApiHeadersNote: For POST /auth/token: X-API-KEY, X-API-SECRET, and X-Tenant-ID (apiKey / tenantId schemes in the contract).
httpApiDoc.errorsBody: Errors return JSON with a code and a human-readable message. HTTP 4xx codes indicate invalid requests; 5xx indicate server errors.
httpApiDoc.schemasIntroBefore: Primary models exposed by the API (overview). Use the full

endpointRoles.ep-get-health: Check that the gateway is up; for load balancers or uptime probes (no auth).
endpointRoles.ep-post-auth: Exchange username/password (Auth body) for a JWT used on protected routes.
endpointRoles.ep-post-auth-token: Exchange API credentials via X-API-KEY, X-API-SECRET, and X-Tenant-ID for a JWT.
endpointRoles.ep-post-payments-legacy: Start a payment through the legacy unified model (deprecated; prefer intention-specific routes).
endpointRoles.ep-get-payments: List and filter the tenant’s payments (pagination, period, status, intention).
endpointRoles.ep-get-payment-by-id: Fetch full detail and status for one payment.
endpointRoles.ep-post-payment-capture: Capture funds on a previously authorized payment (full or partial).
endpointRoles.ep-post-payment-refund: Refund a captured payment (full or partial).
endpointRoles.ep-post-payment-cancel: Cancel a payment that is still cancellable.
endpointRoles.ep-post-payments-cashin: Create a cash-in: inbound funds to the partner account.
endpointRoles.ep-post-payments-cashout: Create a cash-out payout to a destination (bank, wallet, etc.).
endpointRoles.ep-post-payments-transfer: Create a transfer between instruments or to another tenant.
endpointRoles.ep-post-payments-service-payment: Create a service-type payment (e.g. utility, airtime) per contract.
endpointRoles.ep-get-tenant: Read tenant profile and activation status.
endpointRoles.ep-put-tenant-whitelist: Set the IP allowlist for API calls for this tenant.
endpointRoles.ep-get-tenant-api-keys: List API keys for the tenant (metadata only; secrets not returned).
endpointRoles.ep-post-tenant-api-keys: Issue a new API key (SANDBOX/LIVE, scopes).
endpointRoles.ep-get-tenant-api-key: Read one API key’s details (no secret).
endpointRoles.ep-put-tenant-api-key: Update a key’s name or active/inactive status.
endpointRoles.ep-get-tenant-users: List portal users attached to the tenant.
endpointRoles.ep-post-tenant-users: Create a user with a business role (ADMIN, DEVELOPER, FINANCE, VIEWER).
endpointRoles.ep-post-webhooks: Register a URL to receive event callbacks (e.g. payment completed).
endpointRoles.ep-put-webhook: Update URL, event types, or status of a webhook.
endpointRoles.ep-get-reports-transactions: Paginated transactions report for analytics or internal UI.
endpointRoles.ep-get-reports-transactions-export: Export a date range of transactions as CSV.

aboutPage.railsImageAlt: Multichannel collection and how teams monitor it
aboutPage.lede: We orchestrate collections around mobile money (MTN Mobile Money, Orange Money) and card payments, with a single view in the partner portal and API.
aboutPage.p1: MTN MoMo, Orange Money, and card transactions all sit in the same event model in the partner portal and API—product, operations, and finance read the same statuses without a separate workflow per method.
aboutPage.p2: The point is to aggregate these modes instead of running each in its own tool or export—from mass mobile money to card checkout, every path stays traceable for operations and reconciliation.
aboutPage.railsLead: Three major rails at the core of our offer—turn them on and combine them for your market, with end-to-end consistency.
aboutPage.railMtnBody: Collect, fund, and send on the mobile money account tied to MTN: status, amount, and fees show up like any other event on Smallpay, for day-to-day control and API integration.
aboutPage.railOrangeBody: Payments and fund movements on the Orange Money wallet: the same monitoring screens and webhooks, so you do not run this channel in parallel to cards and other rails.
aboutPage.railCardBody: Card acceptance on global networks, alongside mobile money: one place for volume, settlement timing, and chargebacks in the same back office.
aboutPage.v1Body: Statuses and logs read the same way for engineering, support, and finance—fewer cross-team threads to agree on a single payment.
aboutPage.v2Title: MTN, Orange Money, and card on the same rail
aboutPage.v2Body: These methods are not siloed: orchestration is built so MTN MoMo, Orange Money, and card all read the same way in the portal, exports, and the API layer.
aboutPage.v3Body: Portal, HTTP docs, and sandboxes to shape use cases before you switch on production, with the same event model throughout.
aboutPage.trustP1: We work to engineering and data-governance standards aligned with card-scheme requirements and the expectations of our bank and acquirer partners.
aboutPage.trustP2: How we run the platform—controls, logs, access traceability—is designed to be reviewable on your side: you know what to keep for your own internal evidence and compliance conversations.
aboutPage.audience1: Merchants and online brands that want a single place to see collections and settlement.
aboutPage.audience2: Platforms, aggregators, and PSPs onboarding merchants to one back office.
aboutPage.audience3: IT and product teams that integrate by API or start from the web portal, depending on business priority.
